speaker: interviewer
text: And the interviewer is Leonie Lachmish. You were telling me that your parents decided that it was essential to send you to England. Can you tell me about the few days before you actually left Munich? What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very little. Precious little, because everything happened from one day to the next. My mother came home and said, look, there's a place in the Kindertransport. We've decided you will go tomorrow. So tomorrow I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel when she told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A feeling of sadness to leave my parents, but one felt, well, after all, we have our ticket to America, we have our affidavit, our things are in Antwerp, my parents will follow on soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would all meet again in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your sister's reaction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Much the same. My sister harmonized with my mother thought that it wasn't good for her to leave home. I left my mother and father and sister, hoping to meet again in due course.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the day that you actually left, who went with you to the station? Where did you leave from?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left from the main station in Munich. If I remember right, my mother came to see me off, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like many other mothers. I wasn't the only one. There was a group of children. We said goodbye, and we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what your father was doing at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father went to business, like any other day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the farewell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how old were you?

speaker: interviewer
text: 16. Did you go with anyone that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell us a little about the journey itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: I left Munich. We went via Holland. We came to England. For me, it was a totally new experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened on the train itself? Did any Nazis come on to check?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, of course, there was border control and border check, and we had to open our cases. But as I had little to show, it was a simple process.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember how you felt when they came on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we all fell-- felt apprehensive. And we were told to sit quiet and to answer questions when asked and to keep our mouths shut. And that's what we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say you went through Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through Holland. Immediately we crossed the border, we experienced a feeling of relief. We stood on the border for some hours. The train was halted, and started, and halted, and started. But eventually we did cross, and we arrived in Holland. And the first experience for me as a boy was a beautiful white bread that I had never seen before, to eat white bread like the Dutch had.

speaker: interviewer
text: What bread had you been used to eating?

speaker: interviewee
text: A dark or semi-dark simple German bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was in charge of you on this Kindertransport?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a group leader.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who brought you the white bread?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess the committee, whoever was responsible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any particular events in the-- in-- during the train journey at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing special? Were you wearing a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing. We wore--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been wearing a yellow star back in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. We left before the introduction of the yellow star.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Do you remember the date that you left Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was either the 4th of Nove-- the 4th of January. I arrived in England on the 5th. We could have stayed overnight and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And which year are we talking about?

speaker: interviewer
text: 1939. So where--

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived in England on the 5th of January, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you arrive in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Harwich. Hoek van Holland, Harwich.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about the arrival?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken to a large camp. There were many hundreds of children there. Little children, bigger children. And now we began to settle a little. It was actually a disused holiday camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Harwich.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Harwich itself.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Harwich, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dovercourt. In Dovercourt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many-- do you know how many children were on this [? transfer? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would think perhaps 400 or 500 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to this camp, was it already populated by children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were lots of children there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So on your transport, there were around 400, 500 children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. On my [? transfer ?] were possibly 20 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: We collected a few more in Holland who came from other towns, but in Dovercourt, it must have been 400, 500, perhaps 600 children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Probably 90% were Jewish, and the others were non-Jewish, or half Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why would a non-Jew have been with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Political.

speaker: interviewer
text: For example? Can you think of any examples?

speaker: interviewee
text: If their parents were political [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in some ways, the children were sent out.

speaker: interviewer
text: If the-- they objected to Hitler in any way--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they sent the children out. Mhm. How were you treated there in Dovercourt?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was treated fairly well. It was a difficult process for us. First of all, we had to learn the language. Secondly, there were children from two years till 17. The younger children were gradually adopted. People came-- the Jewish community of London, in particular, came, usually on Sundays. And we were all taken out on parade and displayed like, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean you were taken out on parade?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were taken out like chattel and said, look, this is a group of children. Anything that appeals to you here? And then people would say, yes, I like this little girl. Or I'd like that little boy. But when it came to boys of 12, 14, 15, 16, we were too old to be adopted. So as the little children were adopted, we were left behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were these little children officially adopted?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some were adopted officially, and some were just taken in care, to be cared for.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you used to be paraded out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I accepted as it was meant to be. It was meant not as a display of unkind, but of kindness. The Jewish community tried to find homes for us, and it was difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any expectations or hopes that you would be one of the chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: I soon realized, after two or three weeks, there was little hope for me. And so I formulated my plans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What could I do?

speaker: interviewer
text: Meanwhile, did you have connections with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you communicate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Post, there was still post. I got mail regularly, almost every week, and I did write to my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know that they received your letters, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: They received my letters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they writing to you, what sort of things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Simply that to do all the things I was asked to, to keep myself clean and tidy, and to look around and see what I could do to facilitate their coming to England. They wanted to come, at least for the time being, until we could go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you go about trying to help?

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried. I asked all people that I came into contact with, the officials from the refugee organizations, if there was hope for my parents. They took all the particulars-- every child did the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you spend your days in Dovercourt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was plenty to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give us an example of a day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- in the morning there was breakfast, and then there was a certain amount of sport for children who wanted to do. Then there was entertainment, singing, English lessons morning and afternoon. Very often we had cinema shows in the evening. Then there were local families, non-Jewish families, who came and asked us to come to their home. And I befriended a local English customs officer who collected me and took me home. And there was my first introduction to trifle and custard.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And talking of trifle and custard, the food at Dovercourt, was it kosher food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely kasher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish community, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the way of life there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those that wanted to be observant could be observant. There were synagogue facilities, everything laid on for us. And those who did not want were not compelled to join.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a rabbi there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about you? What did you do then, in those circumstances?

speaker: interviewee
text: It became clear to me that for me, the best plan was to swim with the tides of the sea to see what I could do. And very soon I was informed that the Jewish community considered I would be a good farmer. And I was sent to farming training school. But after a few months, I realized-- after a few weeks, I realized it wasn't the life for me. And I made it quite clear this wasn't the life for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Harwell. Harwell is a small village near Oxford.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it's called Nuffield.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939. First from that camp, we moved to a large camp which call-- was called Kitchener Camp. Kitchener Camp was a huge camp, and that absorbed all Jewish refugees coming from-- from Germany. But we only spent a very short time there and then moved on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this all before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: All before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: All before the war. That was early '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you moved around, you still kept in touch with your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my parents, every week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did their letters change in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: As time went by?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't feel war imminent--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: --any more than they had before?

speaker: interviewee
text: --they didn't feel any more war than we. We were all apprehensive. We were all concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a great responsibility for a 17-year-old boy to try and find work for his parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how would you-- you said you [? were transferred. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: I made my views clear to the ladies from the committee who came to our farm center, said I didn't want to be a farmer. I wanted to be a radio mechanic. Radio mechanic, that was a dream, radio mechanic. And so they found a job for me in a town called Portsmouth. I moved to Portsmouth. I was introduced to a Jewish employer, Major Wolf, who had an electrical company. And very soon I integrated into the Jewish community in Portsmouth. I made myself well-known in the synagogue. I met the rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in Portsmouth. I found myself digs, or lodgings, with a docker's family. At that time, we paid in-- not in pounds or in guineas. Yes, I paid a guinea, which was a pound and a shilling for full board with a docker's family, and she looked after me like a mother. I had a job. I cycled to work every morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how much of your pay was that, the guinea?

speaker: interviewee
text: 2/3. I considered myself well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you used to get to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: By cycle. Cycled every morning. It usually rained, so we were soaking wet and worked in wet clothes all day. But it was fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then September 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: After '39, life in Portsmouth became tense. Portsmouth was a prohibited area for aliens. It was the largest port. The whole Royal Navy was there, or most of it. And either in December or January 1940, we were all told-- they were all told, there were four of us. There were four aliens in Portsmouth, young people like myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they all Jews like you?

speaker: interviewee
text: All Jewish. We had to leave. We could no longer reside in Portsmouth. Police came and said, you have so many days to leave. And I came to London.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did it feel as a Jew being told you were now-- were you called an enemy alien?

speaker: interviewee
text: Enemy-- no, I wasn't an enemy alien. Because I basically came to Britain on a Polish passport, not on a German passport. So I was a friendly alien.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh. But your friends who had come from Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were enemy aliens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: All to-- all had to leave. The British police didn't distinguish. You were an alien, an alien.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to London.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to London. I came to London, found myself a job. And with a job, I found myself lodgings, also with a Jewish family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about the contact with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: From then onwards, it became increasingly difficult, increasingly difficult. And letters did pass, or postcards, but with very little. But towards the end of '39, that stopped. Had no further communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last time you heard from your parents was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you still have the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. When I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's a strange feeling. For me, Britain was my country. If they didn't adopt me, I adopted Britain. And when I later on joined the Air Force, I destroyed everything that I had-- all my letters, everything. I have hardly any photographs left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when you look back, can you understand what made you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, stupidity. Or a sense of pride, that I had been given the opportunity to join the elite, the Royal Air Force, and certainly no one-- I didn't want anybody ever to know that I came from Germany or had any contact with the enemy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the Holocaust going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically, no. No. We didn't know-- I didn't know anything. We knew about concentration camps, we knew about-- because soon after, my father was taken to Buchenwald, which was a notorious concentration camp. But all that was not known to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] during the war itself--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was known to me-- it was known to me later on, as we moved from-- that was really much later, in 1944, I was with the Tactical Air Force. And we moved from the first day, from D-Day onwards, from France, Belgium, Holland, as we moved up, we learned there were concentration camps. We didn't know what was happening until after the war when we learned about Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the moment, we're still in 1940 London.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940, we did-- I didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nothing at all.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were working in London. And what happened as-- as the time went by?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in London, and I had-- then there was a blitz, the bombing of London, which a most terrifying experience, [? where ?] London burned all night for weeks on end. Our life was hell.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is 1940?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did the Blitz go on for?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- for some months. Six or eight weeks. Every night, bombers came and dropped their bombs into the fires, and they burnt incessantly. The complete city, the complete dock area, was enshrouded in smoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And whereabouts were you living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very anxious, at the time, to get out of London. And the only feeling I had, to join the army. So I went to volunteer section and volunteered for the army. And no, they wouldn't have me. First of all, I was in reserved occupation. That meant the job I was doing was reserved. And secondly, I had a hammertoe. It's a little toe, it's really nothing, but they wouldn't have me. So I joined the Air Force. And the Air Force willingly accepted me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You joined the Air Force as a volunteer.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a volunteer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where had you been living until then? In London, where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In London. In the southeast London.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Greenwich.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you joined the Air Force.

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined the Air Force.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your origins did not trouble the RAF?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, the Air Force, no. Friendly alien. But I couldn't immediately. I wanted to go, like, tomorrow. But they wouldn't have me because I was reserved occupation. Reserved occupation didn't mean much. It was an expression that you did some war work. And I did, but really, they could have spared me. However, months, months went by until I was called up. And I was actually called in 1942. I had to wait many, many months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it important for you to join the army or Air Force?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to go. I wanted to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew what we were fighting against. I didn't know what we were fighting for. But I certainly knew what we were fighting against. I wanted to do my share. And Britain did their share for me, and I was anxious to do my share for Britain. Simple.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, between 1940 and 1942, did you make any effort to contact your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't. There was no contact at all. None.

speaker: interviewer
text: And all your friends from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally one could sort of put a card through the Red Cross, which went over another country. But basically, there was no communication.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you used to get together with other friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with other kinder from the [? trip? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they all in the same situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you used to talk about together?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were many subjects, many subjects. But basically, my interest was simple-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to join the war and to fight for Britain. They had other interests. Others want to leave Britain, wanted to go to Israel, to Palestine, to New Zealand, to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: But vis a vis their parents, did-- did you surmise together? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We only lived in hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had hope, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you did have hope.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: You genuinely believed that--

speaker: interviewee
text: We genuinely believed. Of course. One also did hear that somebody got out over Holland and they got here, and we didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1942, you joined the Royal Air Force.

speaker: interviewee
text: Air Force.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like every young man, my dream was to be a pilot. That-- that dream soon came to an end. It was a matter of a few days. After I had done my initial training, I had-- we were tested, and I was found to be colorblind.

speaker: interviewer
text: And a pilot--

speaker: interviewee
text: Good examination. They sent me to Blackpool. I was examined by top surgeons. I was colorblind and, as such, unfit to be a pilot. My next job, what could I do to fly? You could be a mechanic, you could be a-- so I joined to be a mechanic. I did my training, became an Air Force flight mechanic. I learned all about engines. I did my test flights, because the Royal Air Force had adopted a wonderful scheme. When you serviced an aircraft and it was taken for test, all the mechanics who worked on that aircraft had to go up with it. Because if it would fall, they would fall down first. [LAUGHS] So we made sure that we did our job well.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But after a while, again, they asked for volunteers. They didn't tell us what for, but I was always anxious, young, to volunteer. I put my name forwards, and I thought we were going to go to Crete, on airborne landings to Crete. No such luck. No such luck. Some weeks afterwards, my name was called on parade, and I was informed that I had been singled out for special duties. What the special duties, they didn't know and I didn't know. I was sent to Cowes-- not to Cowes, to London at first, and I was informed that they had learned of my qualifications that I spoke German. And they wanted to use me for special duties. Then I was seconded to the special section of the intelligent that dealt with information on Germany. We had code and cipher listening devices, everything possible to assist our air force in information. Or airborne, to sit in an aircraft, to fly over, all sorts of things. I did my tests very well. I became a sergeant. From then onwards, of course, I enjoyed the life in the sergeants' mess.

speaker: interviewer
text: From which month are we in, which year now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we're now into '33--

speaker: interviewee
text: '43, '43, forgive me. '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you became a sergeant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Became a sergeant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you stationed?

speaker: interviewee
text: We started in Kingston and Beachy Head-- that's the highest point Eastbourne-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? all ?] [INAUDIBLE] stationed, then we were going on to the end of '43, early '44. Again, they wanted special duty volunteers. I was a sergeant, and I joined the Combined Operations, which are well-known as the Number 4 Commandos for the Royal Air Force. Our duty was to prepare for D-Day. We didn't know when D-Day was to be, but we knew a landing was imminent. I joined the FDT 13-- that was our ship, off Cowes. And we prepared for D-Day. Life was half exciting and sometimes monotonous, because we all had a job to do and prepared for D-Day. Then came D-Day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does D-Day stand for?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-Day is [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the day when we landed, when the British troops, when Britain and American, our allies, landed on the beaches. And that was the 6th of June, '44. 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which beaches are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We landed off Normandy. Our life was hell, but God was kind. And here I am. We came back. There were three ships. The three ships, the FDTs, the Fighter Direction Tender, to direct all the aircraft flying above us. Because beach, a landing without an air force is impossible. And our ships could not land, and that was a sad chapter for me. I wanted to land, but we were laying off the shore. We were laying off the beach. We couldn't move. We were sitting ducks. And after a few days, the Germans realized what we were there for. They could see us, and they came out to sink. Two ships went down, and we came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many were in each ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: Approximately 150, 200 men. All different jobs-- there were war engineers and navy, all different jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have other Jewish sergeants or other soldiers with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes, yes. I met Jewish sergeants. I met Jewish sergeants before the landing and after the landing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you manage to keep any sort of Jewish life in the army-- in the Royal Air Force?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. Very much. The Royal Air Force was wonderful. In 1945, as we moved-- and just imagine our life from Normandy, from-- after, after the landing, we came back and we formed and went back to France. France, Belgium, Holland, Germany, the whole movement up. And as we moved up, and all the-- all the problems that we had. Came Pesach, came Pesach 1945, we came off night duty. I was absolutely dead, called to the office. Sergeant, we have orders. Tonight is your Passover. No flying. All duties crews come off. No pilots, no Jewish pilots go out tonight. And we were taken to a place called Eindhoven. The Royal Air Force had prepared Seder for us. The Royal Air Force found time to fly a Dakota back to England to bring back matzot, maror, haroses. And they laid on a sumptuous Seder evening for us in an empty shop in Nijmegen. 300 Air Force personnel sat down for the first decent meal they've had for weeks. That's beside the point. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A chaplain gave a service, unforgettable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the chaplain was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I remember the catering officer, [? Monnickendam. ?] Flying officer [? Monnickendam ?] from the famous [? Monnickendam ?] bakery family in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was behind all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Royal Air Force. It was all arranged by non-Jewish personnel of the Royal Air Force.
